title: Myzorrhiza vallicola
intro: Myzorrhiza vallicola, commonly known as Valerian root, is a type of plant belonging to the Valerianaceae family, traditionally used for its medicinal and adaptogenic properties. The health benefits of Valerian root include reducing anxiety and insomnia, improving sleep quality, and alleviating symptoms of menopause and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Valerian root include valerenic acid, isovaleric acid, and valepotriates, which contribute to its sedative and anxiolytic effects. Valerian root is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, capsules, and sleep-promoting supplements, often in combination with other herbs like lemon balm and passionflower.
taxonomy: Myzorrhiza vallicola is classified as a species of fungi in the family Gigasporaceae. This fungus is a member of the order Glomerales, within the phylum Glomeromycota. It is a type of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungus, which forms symbiotic relationships with plant roots. Myzorrhiza vallicola is found in Western Australia and is a relatively rare species. The classification of M. vallicola is subject to ongoing research and may be refined as more information becomes available.

side_effects: Myzorrhiza vallicola, also known as a specific type of mycorrhizal fungus, generally does not have direct side effects on humans due to its underground nature. However, the trees or plants it infects may experience stress or reduced growth if the fungus is overcolonizing. In rare cases, the fungus can cause physical damage to plant tissues, but this is typically a result of the fungus outcompeting the plant for resources. There is no documented evidence of Myzorrhiza vallicola causing harm to humans or other animals. Overall, the fungus is considered a beneficial symbiotic organism.
